proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the binary features, several individual features (notably feature_001, feature_014, feature_021) are each positively associated with objective_response, while feature_026 and feature_009 are negatively associated.

analyses.code: for c in binary_cols: chi2_contingency(pd.crosstab(df[c], df['objective_response']))
analyses.result_summary: feature_001=1 raises response 0.165->0.221 (OR 1.435, p=1.9e-55). feature_014=1 raises response 0.167->0.235 (OR 1.526, p=5.8e-70). feature_021=1 raises response 0.166->0.220 (OR 1.413, p=6.6e-51). feature_026=1 lowers response 0.191->0.168 (OR 0.856, p=3.3e-06). feature_009=1 lowers response 0.190->0.166 (OR 0.847, p=3.5e-05). Other binary features (feature_005, feature_010, feature_015, feature_016, feature_018, feature_006, feature_008, feature_019, feature_025, feature_030, feature_032) showed no significant marginal association.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_004 (3-level ordinal) is monotonically associated with lower objective_response as level increases.
Higher feature_024 (continuous, range 20-100) is associated with lower objective_response.
Higher feature_020 (continuous, range 0-23.6) is associated with lower objective_response.
Higher feature_029 (continuous, range 1.7-5.5) is associated with higher objective_response.
Higher feature_022 (continuous WBC-like, range 0.5-500) is associated with lower objective_response.
feature_011 (age-like, range 18-92) is not associated with objective_response.

analyses.result_summary: Response rate falls monotonically with feature_004 level: 0.231 (lvl 0, n=17592), 0.174 (lvl 1, n=24971), 0.129 (lvl 2, n=7437). Chi-square p=1.8e-90; ordinal logistic coef=-0.36 per level (OR 0.70).
feature_024 univariate log-odds slope = -0.0049 per unit (OR_per_SD=0.91, p=2.4e-16). Direction matches hypothesis.
feature_020 slope = -0.0357 per unit (OR_per_SD=0.87, p=1.1e-30). Strong negative association.
feature_029 slope = +0.117 per unit (OR_per_SD=1.06, p=2.9e-07). Modest positive association.
feature_022 slope = -0.00177 per unit (OR_per_SD=0.96, p=1.9e-04). Direction matches hypothesis.
feature_011 slope = -0.00016 per unit (OR_per_SD=1.00, p=0.88). No marginal association detected.

proposed_hypotheses.text: When all 35 features are entered jointly, feature_004, feature_014, feature_001, feature_021 retain the strongest independent associations with objective_response (positive for 014/001/021, negative for 004), and feature_020, feature_024, feature_029, feature_026, feature_009, feature_027, feature_022 retain secondary independent signals.

analyses.result_summary: Multivariable logistic on all features (continuous standardized): feature_004 coef=-0.361 (p=9.1e-93), feature_014 +0.435 (p=1.7e-71), feature_001 +0.373 (p=2.4e-57), feature_021 +0.362 (p=8.2e-54), feature_020 -0.142/SD (p=2.7e-31), feature_024 -0.100/SD (p=1.2e-17), feature_029 +0.061/SD (p=1.4e-07), feature_026 -0.169 (p=6.1e-07), feature_009 -0.188 (p=3.6e-06), feature_027 -0.060/SD (p=5.8e-06), feature_022 -0.048/SD (p=1.3e-04). All other features non-significant after adjustment. Confirms a small set of dominant predictors.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_001, feature_014, and feature_021 act super-additively: response is elevated dramatically only when all three are simultaneously =1, and is near-baseline otherwise (3-way interaction).

analyses.result_summary: Cross-tab response rates by (feature_001, feature_014, feature_021): (0,0,0)=0.172, (1,0,0)=0.165, (0,1,0)=0.160, (0,0,1)=0.162, (1,1,0)=0.160, (1,0,1)=0.169, (0,1,1)=0.157, (1,1,1)=0.633 (n=2401). Seven of eight cells cluster near baseline 0.16-0.17; only the all-ones cell jumps to 63%. Saturated logistic model: 3-way interaction term = +2.13 (p=1.0e-91); all 2-way interactions and main effects for these three features are non-significant once the 3-way is included.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_001, feature_014, and feature_021 are mutually independent in the cohort (no clinical-grouping correlation between them).

analyses.result_summary: Pairwise chi-square independence p-values: f001 x f021 p=0.75, f001 x f014 p=0.48, f014 x f021 p=0.80. The three features are statistically independent. Their super-additive joint effect is therefore not driven by collinearity but by genuine multiplicative interaction.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Define triple_positive = (feature_001=1 AND feature_014=1 AND feature_021=1). Within triple_positive patients, additional binary features modify response. Specifically feature_026=1 and feature_009=1 each suppress response toward baseline, while other binary features have little additional effect.

analyses.code: logistic regression within df[triple==1]; interaction screen triple x feature_x
analyses.result_summary: Within triple_positive (n=2401, baseline rate 0.633): feature_026 coef=-2.78 (OR 0.062, p=5.5e-75); feature_009 coef=-2.67 (OR 0.069, p=1.1e-54); feature_004 coef=-0.50 per level (p=6.3e-11); feature_020 coef=-0.18/SD (p=4.1e-04). Cross-population interaction screen: triple x feature_026 p=1.8e-62, triple x feature_009 p=4.3e-42; no other binary feature shows a significant interaction with triple at p<0.05 after Bonferroni. Both feature_026 and feature_009 collapse the elevated response back to ~0.18.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The complete favorable subgroup is feature_001=1 AND feature_014=1 AND feature_021=1 AND feature_026=0 AND feature_009=0. Patients meeting all five conditions have objective_response near 0.79; all other patients have rate near 0.165.

analyses.result_summary: Favorable subgroup: n=1790 (3.58% of cohort), response rate 0.7860. Non-favorable: n=48210, response rate 0.1653. Chi-square p << 1e-300; rate difference +0.621. Within triple_positive, splitting on (feature_026=0 AND feature_009=0) vs (feature_026=1 OR feature_009=1) gives rates 0.786 vs 0.185 (chi2 p=1.5e-155). Confirms the full 5-feature subgroup as the cleanest treatment-effect rule.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among feature_001, feature_014, feature_021, feature_026, feature_009, all five are mutually independent in the cohort, supporting genuine multiplicative biology rather than collinearity.
Either feature_026=1 OR feature_009=1 alone is sufficient to abolish the favorable effect (the two adverse modifiers act symmetrically and cooperate as an OR-rule rather than an AND-rule).

analyses.result_summary: All pairs non-significant (p>=0.32 across the 10 pairs). f026 vs f009 p=0.32. Independence confirmed for all five subgroup features.

Within triple_positive (n=2401): (f026=0,f009=0) rate=0.786 (n=1790); (f026=0,f009=1) rate=0.187 (n=235); (f026=1,f009=0) rate=0.182 (n=335); (f026=1,f009=1) rate=0.195 (n=41). The presence of either modifier drops response to ~0.18-0.20. Thus the OR-rule (f026=1 OR f009=1) predicts unfavorable outcome within triple_positive, not the AND-rule.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Within the favorable subgroup, feature_004 (3-level ordinal) still inversely predicts response, with rates declining monotonically across levels 0->1->2.
feature_004 modifies the favorable-subgroup effect on the log-odds scale (favorable x feature_004 interaction) - i.e., feature_004 attenuates the favorable effect more than purely additively.

analyses.result_summary: Within favorable subgroup: feature_004=0 rate=0.859 (n=617); =1 rate=0.772 (n=902); =2 rate=0.668 (n=271). Monotonic decline. Chi-square p=4.3e-10.

favorable x feature_004 interaction coef=-0.173 (p=0.050) - borderline. Main effects: favorable +3.13 (p<<0.001), feature_004 -0.384 per level (p<<0.001). The interaction is small relative to main effects; feature_004 acts approximately additively on the log-odds scale, slightly amplifying the response gap as feature_004 rises but mostly an independent main effect.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Within the favorable subgroup, higher feature_020 (peripheral lab measure) reduces response.
Within the favorable subgroup, no other continuous feature shows an independent effect after adjusting for feature_004 and feature_020.

analyses.result_summary: Within favorable subgroup: feature_020 coef=-0.21/SD (p=2.7e-04). Quartile rates: q0=0.81, q1=0.80, q2=0.73 (with sparse highest quartile). Direction confirmed.

Within favorable subgroup top non-feature_004/non-feature_020 predictors: feature_015 p=0.10, feature_029 p=0.05, feature_033 p=0.014. None reach Bonferroni-corrected significance over 35 tests within the small subgroup (n=1790). Only feature_004 (p=6e-11) and feature_020 (p=3e-04) clearly attenuate response within favorable.

proposed_hypotheses.text: If feature_014 (lowest-prevalence of the three positive interactors at ~30% and largest univariate OR=1.53) is treated as the candidate treatment, then feature_001=1, feature_021=1, feature_026=0, feature_009=0 jointly define the responder subgroup; the treatment is null in any patient missing any of those four predicates.
The same conclusion is invariant to which of feature_001/014/021 is labelled treatment - the three are exchangeable in the data due to symmetric 3-way interaction and mutual independence.

analyses.code: for tx in [f001,f014,f021]: scan tx x feature_x interactions across all features
analyses.result_summary: Treatment-effect heterogeneity scan: (a) tx=feature_014: top modifiers feature_001 (+1.06 p=6.8e-103), feature_021 (+1.06 p=5.5e-102), feature_026 (-0.60 p=1.1e-16), feature_009 (-0.47 p=6.8e-08). (b) tx=feature_001: feature_014 (+1.06), feature_021 (+0.88), feature_026 (-0.38), feature_009 (-0.28), all highly significant. (c) tx=feature_021: feature_014 (+1.06), feature_001 (+0.88), feature_009 (-0.41), feature_026 (-0.31). The same four modifiers appear regardless of which is treated as the treatment, confirming exchangeability and the 5-feature subgroup definition.

proposed_hypotheses.text: An exhaustive search over (triple_positive AND two more binary predicates) confirms that adding feature_026=0 AND feature_009=0 yields the highest within-rule response rate among all binary 5-feature rules; no alternative pair improves on this.

Other candidate binary features (feature_010, feature_030, ~45% prevalence, plausibly randomization indicators) show no detectable treatment-effect heterogeneity and no main effect, suggesting they are not operative treatment variables in this cohort.

analyses.code: for c1,c2,v1,v2 in pairs of (binary_feature, value): rate within triple_positive AND (c1==v1) AND (c2==v2) requiring n>=200
analyses.result_summary: Top 5 (rate, n) when added on top of triple_positive: (f009=0, f026=0)->0.786 n=1790; (f018=1, f026=0)->0.750 n=280; (f026=0, f030=0)->0.725 n=1087; (f015=0, f026=0)->0.722 n=1796; (f019=0, f026=0)->0.720 n=1869. The (f009=0 AND f026=0) pair dominates, confirming the canonical 5-feature subgroup.

analyses.code: tx in [f010, f030] x feature_x interaction scan
analyses.result_summary: feature_010 ATE on response: 0.189 vs 0.186 (diff +0.003, p=0.42). feature_030 ATE: 0.189 vs 0.186 (diff +0.003, p=0.47). No significant interactions with any feature at Bonferroni-corrected p<0.001 (best: f010 x f032 p=2e-04, f030 x f026 p=1.5e-03 - none survive correction). These ~45%-prevalence features behave as null variables.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The final treatment-effect subgroup hypothesis: response to whichever of feature_001/014/021 is the operative therapy is concentrated in patients with all of (feature_001=1, feature_014=1, feature_021=1, feature_026=0, feature_009=0). In this subgroup objective_response is ~0.79 (vs ~0.16 elsewhere), an absolute gap of +0.62 and a chi-square p < 1e-300. feature_004 and feature_020 further attenuate response within this subgroup but do not flip its direction. No other binary feature defines an additional layer of subgrouping at population scale.

analyses.code: df['favorable'] = (f001==1)&(f014==1)&(f021==1)&(f026==0)&(f009==0); model with within-subgroup feature_004+feature_020 effects
analyses.result_summary: Final subgroup: n=1790, rate=0.786. Outside: n=48210, rate=0.165. Within-subgroup gradient by feature_004: 0.859 / 0.772 / 0.668 across levels 0/1/2 (chi2 p=4.3e-10). Within-subgroup feature_020 slope -0.21/SD (p=2.7e-04). The favorable-subgroup hypothesis is robustly supported and is the dominant heterogeneity signal in this cohort.
